slides.title: Objects That Arrive Ready
slides.body: Every time you create an object, Python can **automatically run setup code** for you. That magic happens inside a special method called `__init__`.
slides.tip: "init" is short for initialise — it sets up the object the moment it is born.

slides.title: Your First __init__
slides.body: Define `__init__` inside a class to give every new object its own data right away. The arguments you pass at creation flow straight into the method.

slides.title: self = 'This Exact Object'
slides.body: `self` is automatically passed by Python and refers to **the specific instance being created**. Writing `self.name = name` pins the value onto that one object — not all dogs, just this one.
slides.tip: Always name it `self` — it is a rock-solid Python convention every developer expects.

slides.title: Each Instance Is Independent
slides.body: Every object gets its **own copy** of the attributes. Changing one instance never touches another.
slides.code:
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, owner, balance=0):
        self.owner = owner
        self.balance = balance

a = BankAccount("Alice", 100)
b = BankAccount("Bob")
print(a.balance, b.balance)
slides.tip: Default values (like `balance=0`) let callers skip optional arguments.

slides.title: You've Got __init__ Down!
slides.body: Python calls `__init__` **automatically** — you never call it yourself. Use `self.x = value` inside it to store data, and every method in the class can read that data through `self`.
